metadata.summary: AWS service selection decision guide: compute (Lambda vs Fargate vs EKS vs EC2), database (Aurora vs DynamoDB vs ElastiCache), Well-Architected 6 pillars, and agent-model selection per task type.

content.title: AWS Service Selection: Compute, Database, and Architecture Decisions
content.summary: Choosing the right AWS service avoids rework. Compute selection follows: short tasks under 15 minutes use Lambda; container workloads prefer Fargate unless EKS control is needed; sustained CPU workloads use Graviton EC2 Reserved Instances. Database selection follows workload access patterns. The Well-Architected Framework 6 pillars provide the decision checklist across all categories.

why.p: AWS has 200+ services. Selecting the wrong compute or database type at the start of a project creates migration debt that compounds with scale. Graviton instances cost 20% less than equivalent x86 at the same performance. Fargate eliminates node management overhead compared to EKS managed node groups. Aurora Serverless v2 provides sub-second scaling without DBA toil. These decisions are the highest-leverage architectural choices a DevOps engineer makes at project kickoff.

list.item: Starting a new AWS project and selecting core services.
Reviewing an existing architecture for cost or operational efficiency gaps.
Migrating on-premises workloads and mapping legacy components to AWS equivalents.
Conducting a Well-Architected Review against the 6 pillars.
When the architecture is already locked and services are in production — use cost optimization and monitoring methodologies instead.
For service-specific configuration details — see devops-aws-three-tier and devops-aws-serverless-api.

section.name: well-architected
section.p: The AWS Well-Architected Framework defines 6 pillars. Every architecture decision should be evaluated against all 6. The pillar with the most gaps drives the remediation priority.

rule.statement: Every production architecture MUST be evaluated against all 6 Well-Architected pillars: Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, and Sustainability. The evaluation MUST produce a gap list with priority (High/Medium/Low) per pillar before the first production deployment.
rule.rationale: Skipping Well-Architected review at design time results in costly retrofits post-launch. Security and Reliability gaps are much cheaper to fix in Terraform before the first deployment than after data is in production.

section.name: pillars-table
section.p: Pillar summary with focus areas and key practices:

list.item: Operational Excellence: Automation, monitoring, continuous improvement. Key: IaC, runbooks, observability.
Security: IAM, encryption, network security, compliance. Key: least privilege, encryption at rest and in transit.
Reliability: Fault tolerance, recovery, scaling. Key: Multi-AZ, auto-scaling, chaos engineering.
Performance Efficiency: Right-sizing, caching, serverless. Key: Graviton, caching layers, CDN.
Cost Optimization: Reserved capacity, spot instances, right-sizing. Key: Savings Plans, cost allocation tags.
Sustainability: Resource efficiency, managed services. Key: efficient regions, managed services over self-managed.

section.name: compute-selection
section.p: Compute selection is a two-step decision: first choose the execution model (serverless, container, VM), then choose the instance type within that model.

rule.statement: Compute selection MUST follow this priority: tasks under 15 minutes → Lambda (arm64); container workloads without specific EKS requirements → Fargate; container workloads needing node-level control or service mesh → EKS with Graviton managed node groups; batch processing with fault tolerance → AWS Batch with Spot instances; ML inference → Inferentia2 or Graviton. EC2 is a last resort for legacy or specific OS requirements.
rule.rationale: Lambda and Fargate eliminate node management, patching, and capacity planning. Graviton (arm64) costs 20% less than x86 at equivalent performance. Spot instances save up to 90% for interruption-tolerant batch workloads.

list.item: General compute: Graviton (ARM64) instances — default for all new EC2-backed workloads.
Containers: Fargate for simplicity; EKS for node-level control or service mesh requirements.
Short tasks (under 15 minutes): Lambda on arm64.
Batch processing: AWS Batch with Spot instances — up to 90% cost reduction.
ML inference: Inferentia2 or Graviton — purpose-built for inference cost and throughput.

section.name: database-selection
section.p: Database selection follows access patterns, not familiarity with the engine. The most common mistake is defaulting to RDS PostgreSQL when DynamoDB or ElastiCache would be more appropriate.

rule.statement: Database engine selection MUST be driven by access patterns: relational joins and complex queries → Aurora Serverless v2 PostgreSQL; key-value or document with known access patterns → DynamoDB on-demand; sub-millisecond cache → ElastiCache Serverless; graph relationships → Neptune; document store with mongo-compatible API → DocumentDB. Never default to RDS without evaluating DynamoDB first for new greenfield services.
rule.rationale: DynamoDB scales to millions of requests per second with single-digit millisecond latency at any scale, but only when access patterns fit the key-value model. Aurora Serverless v2 handles relational complexity but requires connection management via RDS Proxy at scale.

list.item: Relational data with joins: Aurora Serverless v2 PostgreSQL — scales 0.5 to 128 ACU automatically.
Key-value or single-table access: DynamoDB on-demand — zero capacity planning.
Session cache, leaderboard, rate limiting: ElastiCache Serverless — sub-millisecond, no cluster management.
Document store (MongoDB-compatible): DocumentDB — fully managed, replication built-in.
Graph relationships: Neptune — purpose-built for graph traversal at scale.

section.name: networking-decisions
section.p: Networking decisions follow the traffic flow: internet-facing traffic terminates at CloudFront or ALB; private inter-service traffic stays within the VPC; cross-account traffic uses Transit Gateway or PrivateLink; on-premises connectivity uses Site-to-Site VPN for low bandwidth or Direct Connect for high bandwidth.

list.item: Static assets and global CDN: CloudFront with S3 OAC origin — PriceClass_100 for EU/US.
HTTP API load balancing: Application Load Balancer in public subnets.
TCP/UDP load balancing: Network Load Balancer — static IPs, ultra-low latency.
Global traffic acceleration: Global Accelerator — anycast IPs reduce latency for global users.
AWS service access without NAT: VPC Endpoints (Interface or Gateway type).
Cross-VPC routing: Transit Gateway for 3+ VPCs; VPC Peering for 1:1 connections.

section.name: architecture-patterns
section.p: Standard architecture patterns and their canonical service sets:

list.item: Three-Tier web app: ALB + ECS/EKS + Aurora Serverless v2. See devops-aws-three-tier.
Serverless REST/GraphQL API: API Gateway (HTTP) + Lambda (arm64) + DynamoDB. See devops-aws-serverless-api.
Async event processing: EventBridge + Lambda + SQS with DLQ. See devops-aws-serverless-api.
Microservices: EKS + Service Mesh (App Mesh or Istio) + RDS per service. See devops-aws-three-tier.
Data lake: S3 + Glue + Athena + Redshift Serverless — query data in place.
GenAI RAG: Bedrock + Kendra or OpenSearch Serverless + Lambda + DynamoDB. See devops-aws-serverless-api.

section.name: agent-model-selection
section.p: Different AWS engineering tasks have different cognitive complexity. Selecting the right model tier saves both cost and quality drift.

list.item: Haiku: Run terraform plan, docker build, kubectl get commands — mechanical CLI operations with deterministic output.
Sonnet: Review Dockerfile for best practices, debug pod crashes, write Helm values, troubleshoot Kubernetes pod evictions — code review and diagnosis tasks requiring pattern matching.
Opus: Design multi-region failover architecture, create monitoring strategy for microservices — complex distributed systems decisions requiring reasoning across multiple trade-offs.

section.name: quick-decision-guide
section.p: Quick decision guide — resolve the first matching condition:

list.item: Need serverless compute? Lambda (arm64) + API Gateway + DynamoDB.
Need containers? EKS if node-level control needed; ECS/Fargate otherwise.
Need VMs? EC2 Graviton (legacy or OS-specific requirements only).
Relational data? Aurora Serverless v2 (PostgreSQL or MySQL).
Key-value data? DynamoDB on-demand.
Need cache? ElastiCache Serverless.
Async events? EventBridge + SQS + Lambda.
Real-time streams? Kinesis Data Streams.
Multi-step orchestration? Step Functions.
